skill.name: prometheus-configuration
skill.description: Configure Prometheus metric collection, scrape targets, recording rules, alert rules, validation, and troubleshooting for infrastructure and applications.
skill.summary: Build practical Prometheus monitoring configurations for services, Kubernetes workloads, exporters, rules, and validation workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static command, network, sensitive file, weak crypto, and heuristic findings were reviewed in context. The matches are Markdown examples for Prometheus setup, local API checks, TLS file paths, and alert template syntax. No evidence found of executable code, credential exfiltration, prompt injection, obfuscation, or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Documentation Contains Shell Command Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes Helm, promtool, and curl commands that users may run during Prometheus setup or validation. These are legitimate operational examples, but users should review targets and namespaces before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are inside Markdown code blocks and align with the skill purpose. There is no evidence that the skill executes them automatically.

low_findings.title: Network URLs Are Setup and Localhost Examples
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to the official Prometheus community Helm chart repository and localhost Prometheus API endpoints. No external exfiltration endpoint or encoded data transfer was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL contexts are clear Prometheus installation and validation examples. I found no credential payloads or suspicious remote collection service.

low_findings.title: TLS Key References Are File Paths Only
low_findings.description: The certificate and key findings refer to example Prometheus TLS configuration paths. The file does not contain private key material, certificates, or embedded secrets.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The lines are YAML path references under tls_config. No secret values or credential contents are present in the skill file.

low_findings.title: Static Heuristic Was Triggered By Documentation Patterns
low_findings.description: The critical combination heuristic combines command snippets, URLs, and TLS path references. In context, these are Prometheus documentation examples without executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious elements exist but are not wired together as code. I found no obfuscation, hidden execution path, or instruction to bypass review.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run Infrastructure Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill may guide users to run Helm, promtool, and curl commands. This is expected for Prometheus operations, but the commands affect local or cluster monitoring environments.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code blocks contain real commands that a user could copy. They are transparent and relevant, so the risk is operational rather than malicious.

dangerous_patterns.title: Prometheus TLS Path Configuration
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill shows TLS certificate and key file path fields. This is normal Prometheus configuration, but users must avoid sharing real private key contents.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The TLS fields are present in a scrape configuration example. The finding is low risk because only placeholder filesystem paths are shown.

content.value_statement: Prometheus setup is error-prone when scrape targets, rules, and validation steps are scattered. This skill helps create focused monitoring configurations and review them for common gaps.
actual_capabilities: Design Prometheus scrape configurations for static targets and Kubernetes service discovery.
Create recording rules for request rates, error rates, latency, CPU, memory, and disk usage.
Draft alert rules with severity labels, thresholds, durations, and annotations.
Explain Helm, Docker Compose, and configuration file setup patterns for Prometheus.
Provide validation and troubleshooting steps using promtool and Prometheus API checks.
limitations: It does not run Prometheus, Helm, promtool, or curl commands automatically.
It cannot verify live targets without access to the user's monitoring environment.
It provides examples that must be adapted for real hostnames, ports, labels, and retention needs.
It does not include the referenced supporting files in this package.

use_cases.title: Bootstrap Service Monitoring
use_cases.description: Create scrape jobs, labels, and validation steps for a new application metrics endpoint.

use_cases.title: Standardize Kubernetes Discovery
use_cases.description: Review Kubernetes annotation-based discovery rules before rolling them into a cluster monitoring stack.

use_cases.title: Improve Alert Quality
use_cases.description: Turn raw PromQL expressions into recording rules and alerts with useful severity labels.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Scrape Jobs
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup for a few static exporters.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Prometheus scrape configuration for three node exporters and one application endpoint. Include labels and validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Add Kubernetes Service Discovery
prompt_templates.prompt: Design Kubernetes service discovery rules for annotated services. Explain each relabeling step and how to test target discovery.

prompt_templates.title: Build Recording And Alert Rules
prompt_templates.prompt: Create recording rules and alert rules for request rate, error rate, latency, CPU, memory, and disk saturation.

prompt_templates.title: Review Production Prometheus Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Prometheus plan for retention, scrape intervals, TLS settings, rule groups, and failure modes. Flag risky assumptions.

output_examples.input: I need monitoring for two API servers and three Linux hosts.
output_examples.output: A structured plan with scrape jobs, target labels, metric paths, retention guidance, and validation commands to run locally.

output_examples.input: My team needs alerts for latency, errors, and resource saturation.
output_examples.output: A rule design that separates recording rules from alerts, with thresholds, durations, labels, and clear alert annotations.

output_examples.input: I want to check if my Kubernetes discovery rules are correct.
output_examples.output: A relabeling review that explains which annotations are required and how to confirm discovered targets in Prometheus.

best_practices: Keep scrape labels consistent across environments, services, and exporters.
Use recording rules for expensive or repeated PromQL expressions.
Validate configuration and rules before applying them to production Prometheus instances.
anti_patterns: Do not copy example hostnames, ports, or TLS paths without adapting them.
Do not create alerts without clear severity, duration, and action context.
Do not set retention or scrape intervals without checking storage and query load.

faq.question: Does this skill install Prometheus automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides setup guidance and command examples that users must review and run themselves.

faq.question: Can it create Kubernetes scrape configurations?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers annotation-based Kubernetes service and pod discovery patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with alert rules?
faq.answer: Yes. It can draft alert rules with thresholds, durations, labels, and annotations.

faq.question: Does it include Grafana dashboards?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on Prometheus configuration, scraping, recording rules, alerts, and validation.

faq.question: Can it validate my live Prometheus server?
faq.answer: Only if the agent has access to your environment and you approve any needed commands.

faq.question: Is it suitable for production use?
faq.answer: Yes, as a configuration aid. Production settings still need environment-specific review and testing.
